critic_raw: The YNet trajectory forecasting replication has two primary concerns. First, the segmentation maps used as scene context are preprocessed from bird's-eye view camera footage — the segmentation quality depends critically on camera placement and calibration. In the Stanford Drone Dataset, scenes are recorded from high above, producing consistent perspective, but ETH/UCY scenes have more variable camera heights. The replication does not document whether the same segmentation preprocessing pipeline was applied consistently across both datasets, introducing a potential source of result variability. Second, the evaluation metric is Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Final Displacement Error (FDE) with k=20 multi-modal samples. This setup rewards methods that generate diverse predictions — a method that generates wildly inaccurate but diverse samples would score well on minADE. The replication reports top-k metrics without noting this diversity-accuracy tradeoff or providing any NLL-based metric that penalizes overconfident or underconfident predictions.
all_issues_raised: Segmentation preprocessing pipeline consistency across SDD and ETH/UCY datasets not documented
minADE/minFDE evaluation rewards diversity without penalizing accuracy — NLL metric absent

round_transcripts.critic: The YNet trajectory forecasting replication has two primary concerns. First, the segmentation maps used as scene context are preprocessed from bird's-eye view camera footage — the segmentation quality depends critically on camera placement and calibration. In the Stanford Drone Dataset, scenes are recorded from high above, producing consistent perspective, but ETH/UCY scenes have more variable camera heights. The replication does not document whether the same segmentation preprocessing pipeline was applied consistently across both datasets, introducing a potential source of result variability. Second, the evaluation metric is Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Final Displacement Error (FDE) with k=20 multi-modal samples. This setup rewards methods that generate diverse predictions — a method that generates wildly inaccurate but diverse samples would score well on minADE. The replication reports top-k metrics without noting this diversity-accuracy tradeoff or providing any NLL-based metric that penalizes overconfident or underconfident predictions.
round_transcripts.defender: The segmentation preprocessing pipeline is identical for both datasets — it uses the same semantic segmentation model applied to the provided scene maps, not to individual frames. The replication uses the provided preprocessed scene maps from the original codebase, ensuring consistency. On minADE/minFDE: these are the standard evaluation metrics for probabilistic trajectory forecasting used universally in the field, including by all compared baselines. Critiquing the metric choice is a critique of the field's evaluation standard, not of this specific paper or replication. The replication correctly matches the original evaluation protocol.

round_transcripts.critic: On segmentation: the defense clarifies that preprocessed scene maps from the codebase are used — this is helpful information that the replication paper should document explicitly. The source and version of these maps should be cited to ensure future replicators use identical inputs. On minADE/minFDE: the defense is correct that these are field-standard metrics, which resolves that specific concern. However, the broader point stands: the replication does not note that minADE/minFDE have known diversity-accuracy tradeoff limitations, which would be useful context for future work building on this benchmark.
round_transcripts.defender: Noting field-standard metric limitations is editorial commentary beyond a replication's scope. The replication documentation follows standard practice. Scene map sources are referenced in the codebase.

all_issues_adjudicated: Scene map source and version not cited in replication text — reproducibility requires this documentation
